behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS/REAL-INFRA: this failure is at the watcher↔OS boundary (recursive watch behavior), not just local parsing. The behavior text explicitly requires real filesystem event coverage for nested paths. MOCK-HIDE: in-process fake events can pass even if the real watcher is not subscribed recursively. Rejected lower level: Integration is disqualified by "placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root" because recursion support depends on actual watcher/OS semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file into that subdirectory, and assert the transformed output appears in the output directory without restart.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: behavior is primarily one local dedup state/time-window decision for the same path. REAL-INFRA: no real OS/network/subprocess is required to reproduce this logic bug. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejected higher level: System is disqualified by "deduplication logic" and "same file path within a configurable time window"; this can be reproduced with a local object + controlled clock.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the dedup component with a known window and fake clock, submit the same path twice within the window, assert the second request is dropped, then advance clock past window and assert the next request is accepted.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: behavior text explicitly says "operating system emits multiple filesystem events". MOCK-HIDE: synthetic single-callback mocks can hide duplicate-event burst/race behavior from real watcher backends. Integration-brake (a) is yes (real OS event semantics), so upgrade to System.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory, perform a write pattern that triggers duplicate OS events (e.g., temp-write + rename or multiple flushes), and assert exactly one pipeline execution/output artifact for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: directory deletion/recreation and watcher reattachment are OS-level behaviors. MOCK-HIDE: fake watcher APIs typically do not model invalidated OS watch handles and re-subscription edge cases. Rejected lower level: Integration is disqualified by "directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running" because correctness depends on real filesystem/watch lifecycle.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real directory, delete that directory while daemon stays alive, recreate it at the same path, add a new file, and verify it is processed successfully without restarting the process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: ordering spans watcher events + stat metadata + queue orchestration (module handoff). REAL-INFRA: creation timestamp availability/resolution and event interleaving are filesystem/OS-dependent; mocks can hide ties/granularity issues. Adjacent tie-breaker (Integration vs System): higher level chosen because concrete failure only System catches is incorrect ordering caused by real filesystem timestamp resolution under near-simultaneous creates.
behaviors.test_description: Create several files near-simultaneously in a real watched directory, record their filesystem creation timestamps, run daemon, and assert processing/output order matches timestamp order (including deterministic tie-break policy if timestamps are equal).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: requires real write timing/flush behavior and real watcher event timing. MOCK-HIDE: in-process fakes often deliver only "fully written" fixtures, masking partial-read races. Rejected lower level: Integration is disqualified by "still being written to when the arrival event fires" because the failure manifests from real OS I/O timing.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on a real directory, begin a slow/chunked write to a file, trigger arrival event naturally, assert daemon does not process partial content, then finish write and assert processing occurs once with complete content.
